output:
# RE-MASK: Revisable, In-Place Tool-Use Planning via Re-Maskable Blank-Infilling

**High-level insight (Machine Learning / agentic LLMs).** The GLM lineage is distinguished from
decoder-only GPT/LLaMA models by its **autoregressive blank-infilling** objective, which natively
fills `[MASK]` spans conditioned on bidirectional context and in *arbitrary order*. Meanwhile, the
agentic frontier (GLM-4 All Tools, ReAct, Toolformer, Reflexion, Tree-of-Thoughts) builds tool-use
traces **left-to-right and append-only**: once a tool call, argument, or intermediate result is
committed, it is frozen. We connect these two threads. **RE-MASK** treats the tool-use trace itself
as an *in-place editable, re-maskable artifact* — emitting a plan skeleton of typed `[MASK]` blanks
(tool, args, pending-result) and **reopening and refilling committed slots in non-monotonic order as
live execution feedback arrives**. Append-only agents and external-regeneration agents fall out as the
two degenerate boundary cases of a single revisable-decoding spectrum.

*Epistemic labels are used throughout:* **[provable-by-construction]** (true by definition/reduction),
**[designed]** (an engineering choice we commit to), **[conjectured / design-goal-to-measure]** (a
hypothesis the experiments must confirm or falsify).

---

## [Question 1] - What is the problem?

Can an agent that represents its tool-use plan as a blank-infilling skeleton revise tool calls, arguments, and pending-result slots in place and in non-monotonic order as execution feedback arrives — rather than only appending corrections or externally regenerating the entire trace — and does this re-maskable in-place revision measurably reduce error propagation and context bloat while improving task success?

---

## [Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?

Solving non-monotonic, in-place tool-use revision matters because it names and operationalizes a desideratum the agentic-LM literature has structurally overlooked: **cheap, local revisability of a committed plan**. Today's dominant paradigms optimize *forward* generation quality — ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) and Toolformer (Schick et al., 2023) interleave reasoning and tool calls left-to-right; Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023) appends critiques; Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) and Tree-of-Thoughts (Yao et al., 2023) improve by discarding and re-sampling entire traces or branches. None can reach back and edit a single committed slot — a tool name, an argument, or a pending-result placeholder — in place. RE-MASK reframes the trace itself as an **in-place editable, re-maskable artifact**, exposing *edit-locality* as a first-class axis distinct from final-answer accuracy.

**Implications for future research.** (1) *A new evaluation axis* [conjectured / design-goal-to-measure]: agent benchmarks — AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023), ToolBench/ToolLLM (Qin et al., 2023), API-Bank (Li et al., 2023), Gorilla (Patil et al., 2023) — score only *final* success and are therefore *blind* to revision behavior; RE-MASK motivates metrics for revision count, edit-locality, and wasted-call rate, currently unmeasured. (2) *Reframing agentic decoding* [designed]: it recasts append-only and external-regeneration agents as two degenerate endpoints of a single revisable-decoding spectrum [provable-by-construction], giving the community a unifying lens rather than a zoo of scaffolds. (3) *Bridging two literatures* [conjectured]: it connects the infilling/edit-decoding lineage — GLM blank-infilling (Du et al., 2022), Fill-in-the-Middle (Bavarian et al., 2022), Insertion Transformer (Stern et al., 2019), Levenshtein Transformer (Gu et al., 2019) — with closed-loop tool agents, a coupling neither side has made.

**Advancing knowledge and practice.** Practically, in-place revision targets the dominant failure modes of long-horizon agents: *error propagation* (a bad early result poisons every downstream step), *context bloat* (append-only traces grow monotonically, inflating cost and latency), and *brittleness* to transient tool errors. By editing the offending slot rather than re-running the whole trace, RE-MASK is hypothesized to deliver [conjectured / design-goal-to-measure] *fewer wasted tool calls, smaller working context, and lower latency/cost* — benefits most acute precisely where compute is scarcest, e.g., on-device or small-footprint ChatGLM-6B-class agents. It also enables a capability append-only agents lack by construction: filling a result slot whose value depends on a *later*-decided tool, i.e., genuinely non-monotonic planning. The knowledge contribution is conceptual as much as empirical — it identifies *revisability* as the right optimization target for long-horizon tool use and supplies both a mechanism (native re-masking) and a measurement program for it.

---

## [Question 3] - Why is it hard?

The core difficulty is that **in-place revision of a generated plan is not a native primitive of left-to-right decoders**, and every layer of the problem — model semantics, training data, credit assignment, control, coherence, and evaluation — resists a naive fix.

**(a) Naively prompting a decoder-only model to "edit" fails [provable-by-construction].** Standard autoregressive models (ReAct/Toolformer-style) only define a distribution over the *next* token given a fixed left context. There is no in-place "replace this earlier slot" operation: the only way to change a committed token is to either append a correction (which leaves the wrong value in context — the source of error propagation) or discard and regenerate the suffix (Reflexion/ToT), which is expensive and non-local. Even if you prompt "edit step 3," the model has no learned semantics for *rewriting* an interior span conditioned on later content — and mechanically, mutating an interior token **invalidates the KV-cache** for everything after it, forcing recomputation and breaking the illusion of a cheap local edit. The "edit" is therefore neither cheap nor well-defined under a purely left-to-right objective.

**(b) No editable-trace training corpus exists [provable-by-construction].** Public agent datasets (ToolBench, API-Bank, AgentBench) contain *successful or appended* trajectories, not *minimal in-place repairs*. There is no supervision of the form "given this faulty slot and this later feedback, here is the smallest re-mask + refill that fixes it." Without such targets, a model cannot learn *localized* revision and will default to whole-suffix regeneration.

**(c) Edit-level credit assignment is non-trivial [conjectured].** Whole-trajectory reward (did the task succeed?) is far too coarse to teach *which* slot revision mattered. A trajectory can succeed despite a useless re-mask, or fail despite a correct one. Attributing success to a *single localized* edit requires a counterfactual signal (success delta with vs. without that specific re-mask), which standard RLHF reward shaping does not provide out of the box.

**(d) Non-monotonic slot scheduling can thrash or oscillate [conjectured].** Once a model *can* reopen any slot at any time, an unconstrained controller may re-mask the same slot repeatedly, ping-pong between two candidate values, or chase feedback in loops — converting flexibility into non-termination. The scheduling policy (which blank to (re)open next) is itself a hard sequential decision problem that needs an explicit budget / monotone-progress guard.

**(e) Re-masking must preserve global coherence and tool-call validity [conjectured].** A local edit is not truly local in *meaning*: changing one tool's argument can invalidate a downstream slot that consumed its result, or break schema/type validity of a later call. The system must propagate constraints (dataflow between result slots and consuming calls) without regenerating everything — i.e., decide the *blast radius* of an edit rather than assuming it is one token.

**(f) Evaluation must measure revision behavior, not just final success [provable-by-construction].** Final accuracy alone cannot distinguish RE-MASK from an append-only baseline that simply tries harder. The differentiating claims (less error propagation, less context bloat, more localized repairs) require *new* metrics — edit locality, context-bloat ratio, error-propagation depth, revisions-to-success — and a falsification ladder, none of which existing benchmarks report.

**(g) Safe, valid execution during data collection and evaluation [designed].** Because the loop runs real Python execution and web browsing, every tool call during training and evaluation is run inside an **isolated sandbox** (no network egress beyond designated APIs, no persistent filesystem writes; destructive or irreversible action types are excluded from the action space by design), and web-browsing traces are **post-processed to strip PII** (names, emails, user-identifying URLs) before any dataset release. Because GLM-4 self-bootstraps both injected faults and candidate repairs, a **≥5% human-annotated sample** plus a **non-GLM cross-family judge** validate trace quality to guard against in-family reward hacking.

**Why it is nonetheless tractable.**
1. **The mechanism needs no architecture change [provable-by-construction].** GLM/ChatGLM is *blank-infilling-native*: its pretraining objective already fills `[MASK]` spans conditioned on bidirectional context, in arbitrary order. RE-MASK exploits this existing capability via objective + decoding control, not new weights — sidestepping obstacle (a) for an open, reproducible backbone.
2. **Controlled fault-injection manufactures the missing supervision [designed].** Injecting a wrong arg/tool/order into a *known-good* trace yields a deterministic ground-truth minimal repair, directly producing the editable-trace corpus (b) and a clean counterfactual for edit-level reward (c). Train/test tool-and-API disjointness plus hidden injection sites give a Leakage control.
3. **Boundary cases are built-in sanity checks [provable-by-construction].** Because ReAct = RE-MASK filled strictly left-to-right with zero re-masking, and Reflexion/ToT = RE-MASK that re-masks all downstream blanks, the *Recovery* test (re-masking disabled must reproduce ReAct) and the *Placebo* test (random re-masking must not help) are guaranteed-meaningful checks that constrain (d) and validate (f).

---

## [Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?

The capability has been blocked by a combination of **structural limits in each prior cluster**, an **architectural barrier**, and **evaluation blindness** — no single line of work sat at the intersection RE-MASK occupies.

**Append-only agents cannot edit in place — structurally.** ReAct (Yao et al., 2022), Toolformer (Schick et al., 2023), and Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023) generate strictly left-to-right and append-only. A correction is always *new tokens after* the mistake; the committed trace is immutable. There is no operator in these methods to reopen and overwrite a prior slot, so error propagation and context growth are baked in. RE-MASK improves on this by making the committed slot itself mutable: re-masking reopens it to `[MASK]` and refills in place — and append-only generation falls out as the degenerate case where every blank is filled once, left-to-right, with zero re-masking [provable-by-construction].

**External-regeneration loops regenerate rather than localize.** Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) and Tree-of-Thoughts (Yao et al., 2023) do achieve improvement, but only by discarding and re-sampling whole traces or branches — global, expensive, and non-local. They never perform a *localized* slot edit. RE-MASK subsumes them as the opposite degenerate case: re-mask *all* downstream blanks and refill from scratch [provable-by-construction]; the contribution is the cheap *intermediate* — editing one slot, not the whole plan.

**Infilling/edit-decoders never closed the loop.** This is the closest mechanistic prior art and the **sharpest novelty boundary**. GLM blank-infilling (Du et al., 2022), Fill-in-the-Middle (Bavarian et al., 2022), Insertion Transformer (Stern et al., 2019), Levenshtein Transformer (Gu et al., 2019), and diffusion/edit text models (SUNDAE; CoEdIT, Raheja et al., 2023) can all infill or revise spans — but over *static text or code*. None condition the infilled value on **live tool-execution feedback**, and none reopen a previously committed slot as part of a *closed agentic loop*. The novel coupling is exactly this: the value to infill depends on a result that only exists after a tool runs.

**The architectural barrier.** Decoder-only GPT/LLaMA models are left-to-right and append-biased; bidirectional `[MASK]` infilling is awkward to drive in them, which is why agent frameworks built on them defaulted to append-only or external regeneration. GLM is **blank-infilling-native** (Du et al., 2022), making non-monotonic, in-place slot revision a first-class decoding mode rather than bolted-on scaffolding. The enabling condition simply wasn't exploited for tool use before.

**The broader barriers.** (1) *No editable-trace corpus*: training data and benchmarks record final traces, not the *edit sequences* that produced them, so models were never supervised to revise. (2) *No edit-level credit assignment*: RL/feedback for agents rewards final success, giving no signal for which *slot* to revise. (3) *Evaluation blindness*: AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023), ToolBench (Qin et al., 2023), API-Bank (Li et al., 2023), and Gorilla (Patil et al., 2023) score only the final answer, so the in-place-revision gap was literally unmeasured — there was no metric pressure to solve it. A further confounder we explicitly control for is *benchmark contamination*: because GLM-4's pretraining corpus likely overlaps these public tool benchmarks, the Leakage rung (Q5) is designed to probe **base-model memorization**, not only in-context plan leakage, with an accompanying contamination analysis so reported gains are attributable to RE-MASK rather than recall.

**Defensible novelty boundary.** *RE-MASK is the first to treat the tool-use trace as an in-place editable, re-maskable artifact whose blanks are filled and reopened in non-monotonic order under live tool feedback — unifying append-only (ReAct/Toolformer) and external-regeneration (Reflexion/ToT) agents as its two degenerate boundary cases* [provable-by-construction].

**Strongest objections and rebuttals (honesty guard).** *Objection 1 — "This is just constrained decoding or re-prompting with a template."* Rebuttal: re-masking is a native, model-internal reopening of a committed slot, not an external regenerate-and-splice; the boundary-case proof shows that template re-prompting is precisely the external-regeneration degenerate case RE-MASK generalizes beyond. *Objection 2 — "Infilling LMs already do in-place edits, so this is incremental."* Rebuttal: every cited infilling/edit decoder operates on static text/code; none closes the loop with live tool results, and that closed loop — not the infilling primitive — is the contribution. We label the magnitude of the *empirical* gains [conjectured] pending measurement, while the boundary-case unification is [provable-by-construction], keeping the novelty claim precise and non-overstated.

---

## [Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?

**Backbone & representation.** Use an **open GLM-4 / ChatGLM3-or-4** model as a *blank-infilling* engine [provable-by-construction: blank-infilling is native to GLM]. A tool-use plan is represented as a **`[MASK]`-slot skeleton**: typed blanks for `tool`, `args`, and `pending-result` slots, plus dataflow edges linking a result slot to the calls that consume it. Generation = filling blanks; the **re-mask operator** reopens any committed slot back to `[MASK]` for in-place refill.

**Controller (decoding control).** A **feedback-conditioned slot-selection head** decides *which* blank to (re)open next given the newest tool result, replacing fixed left-to-right order with a learned non-monotonic schedule [designed]. The controller's pre-RL supervision is the **fault-injection oracle itself**: the known faulty slot in each synthesized example provides the ground-truth "which slot to reopen" label, so slot-selection is first learned by supervised imitation before any RL. A **re-mask budget** and monotone-progress guard prevent thrash/oscillation [designed]. On edit, only the affected slot and its dataflow-downstream consumers are recomputed (bounded blast radius), containing KV-cache invalidation cost [conjectured].

**Two-stage training (SFT-primary, RL-secondary).**
1. **SFT on minimal-edit repair traces (primary).** From *successful* ToolBench/API-Bank traces, inject controlled faults (wrong arg / wrong tool / wrong order), then record the **minimal re-mask + refill** that repairs each, optionally bootstrapped with **GLM-4 self-revisions** [designed]. This teaches localized, in-place repair semantics and carries the core claim.
2. **RL with edit-level counterfactual reward (optional/secondary).** Reward = task success **plus** a contrastive/counterfactual term = success delta *with vs. without* the specific re-mask, giving edit-level advantage rather than whole-trajectory credit [conjectured], regularized by the re-mask budget. We treat this stage as an *enhancement*, not a load-bearing component: because counterfactual rollouts are expensive and high-variance, the central result rests on SFT + the ablation ladder, with RL reported as an upper-bound-seeking add-on.

**Safety & integrity of the pipeline [designed].** All tool calls during training and evaluation run inside an **isolated sandbox** (no network egress beyond designated APIs, no persistent filesystem writes; destructive/irreversible actions excluded by design). Web-browsing traces are **PII-scrubbed** before any release. Self-bootstrapped traces are validated by a **≥5% human-annotated sample** and a **non-GLM cross-family judge** to guard against in-family reward hacking. Fine-tuned checkpoints undergo **staged responsible-release evaluation** before publication, and downstream fine-tuners are advised to re-evaluate safety alignment alongside capability gains.

**Data (named, real).**
- **ToolBench / ToolLLM** (Qin et al., 2023) — primary multi-tool task source and trace seeds.
- **API-Bank** (Li et al., 2023) — multi-call planning + retrieval; API-disjoint train/test splits for the Leakage control.
- **AgentBench** (Liu et al., 2023) — cross-environment / OOD generalization slice.
- **Synthesized fault-injection + minimal-repair edit corpus** derived from the above (the supervised edit targets) [designed].

**Metrics (revision quality, not just final success).** Task success rate (pass@1 / pass@k); **error-propagation depth** (downstream steps corrupted before repair); **context-bloat ratio** (effective ÷ accumulated tokens); **edit locality** (tokens re-masked per repair); **revisions-to-success**; **tool-call efficiency** (number of calls, dollar/latency cost). Latency and cost are reported **end-to-end**, explicitly including KV-cache invalidation and blast-radius recompute triggered by interior edits, so the context-bloat advantage is not claimed on token count alone.

**Baselines (same GLM-4 backbone — capacity controlled, mechanism isolated).** ReAct, Reflexion, Tree-of-Thoughts, and GLM-4 All Tools (append-only). Holding the backbone fixed isolates the blank-infilling / re-masking mechanism from raw capability.

**Evaluation ladder (explicit).**
- **Oracle** — gold/oracle slot edit; upper bound on achievable headroom.
- **Placebo** — *random* re-masking; **must not** help (falsifier for "re-masking is just extra compute").
- **Recovery** — re-masking **disabled**; must reproduce ReAct numbers (boundary-case sanity, provable-by-construction).
- **Leakage** — fault-injection sites hidden from the editor; train/test tools & APIs disjoint; plus base-model **contamination analysis** to separate genuine revision gains from memorization.

**Expected outcomes (epistemic-labeled).**
- Higher task success at **lower end-to-end context cost** (improved success with reduced context-bloat ratio and fewer tool calls) [conjectured].
- **Reduced error-propagation depth** vs. append-only baselines, via localized in-place repair [conjectured].
- **Recovery reproduces ReAct** when re-masking is disabled [provable-by-construction].
- **Placebo (random re-mask) does not help**, isolating the value of *targeted* revision [conjectured — designed to falsify].
- **Oracle bounds the headroom**, quantifying how much of the gap learned slot-selection closes [provable-by-construction that it is an upper bound; the *size* of the gap is conjectured].
- Boundary-case unification (ReAct = single-pass left-to-right fill; Reflexion/ToT = full-downstream re-mask) holds by construction [provable-by-construction].
